params.text:
People's rights over their information

UK GDPR gives individuals rights over the information held about them. The ones you're most likely to meet in care are:

  • Right to be informed – people should know what information you hold and why, usually through a privacy notice.
  • Right of access – people can ask to see the information you hold about them. This is a subject access request (SAR).
  • Right to rectification – people can ask you to correct information that is wrong or incomplete.
  • Right to erasure – people can ask you to delete information in certain circumstances (this right is not absolute, especially where records must be kept for care or legal reasons).

Handling a subject access request

A SAR can be made verbally or in writing, by any staff member, and even through social media — the person does not have to use a special form or say the words 'subject access request'. Because of this, you need to recognise one when it happens and pass it on.

Organisations must normally respond within one month, and usually free of charge. If a resident says, "I want to see everything you've written about me," that is a SAR — report it to your manager promptly so it can be handled correctly and on time.

params.question: A subject access request must always be made in writing on an official form before it counts.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. There is no set format: people can make a subject access request verbally or in writing, so any such request must be taken seriously.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. A SAR can be made verbally or in writing, in any format — so you must recognise and act on it however it arrives.
